revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:ابوشی]][[fi:Eboshi]][[ja:烏帽子]][[ru:Эбоси]][[uk:Ворона шапка]][[zh:烏帽子]]
[[File:Yasaka-jinja 01.jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Eboshi|qq=}}]] An '''Eboshi (烏帽子)''' is a type of {{ill|hat|en|hat|qid=Q80151|lt=hat|1=Hat}} traditionally worn by adult [[:en:men|men]] during formal occasions in {{ill|Japanese clothing|en|Japanese clothing|qid=Q1477300|lt=Japanese traditional dress|1=Japanese clothing}} from the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} to the modern era. == Overview == [[File:Eboshi kabuto 1.JPG|thumb|240px|Eboshi-shaped {{ill|Kabuto|en|Kabuto|qid=Q1720878|lt=Kabuto|1=Kabuto}} (helmet)]] Initially, it was made of su11le materials such as black-lacquered {{ill|Silk|en|Silk|qid=Q37681|lt=Silk|1=Silk}} or {{ill|Hemp|en|Hemp|qid=Q7150699|lt=Hemp|1=Hemp}}, but by the late Heian period, {{ill|Paper|en|Paper|qid=Q11472|lt=Paper|1=Paper}} hardened with black lacquer replaced these materials.<ref>{{Cite book |author=Heibonsha Editorial Department |title=Shinpan Nihonshi Mono Jiten (New Edition: Dictionary of Japanese History and Objects) |publisher=Heibonsha |year=2017-06-21 |page=160 |isbn=9784582124293|qq=}}</ref> Commoners used woven hemp thread. The type of eboshi varied based on the formality of the attire and the wearer's social Rank, with strict distinctions maintained. The eboshi is considered less formal than the {{ill|kanmuri|en|kanmuri|qid=Q48814522|lt=kanmuri|1=Kanmuri}} (冠) worn for court attire and was paired with informal wear from the Heian to {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. Some theories suggest its origin lies in the {{ill|Chinese|en|Chinese|qid=Q1173004|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese}} 烏帽.<ref>Fuji TV "Konya wa Nazotore" (2020)</ref> <gallery> File:魏晉朝烏帽.jpg|Eboshi from the [[Wei Jin Southern and Northern Dynasties|Wei Jin era]] File:단석산신라인공양상.jpg|Eboshi seen in the offerings by {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} people, depicted in the bas-reliefs of {{ill|Gyeongju|en|Gyeongju|qid=Q42097|lt=Gyeongju|1=Gyeongju}} Danseoksan Mountain in the early 7th century. </gallery> Originally designed for men, [[:en:Women|Women]] such as {{ill|shirabyōshi|en|shirabyōshi|qid=Q958693|lt=shirabyōshi|1=Shirabyōshi}} (female dancers) also wore eboshi in some cases. From the Heian period onward, it gradually became popular among commoners. By the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura period}} to early Muromachi period, not wearing headgear was considered shameful. For instance, the "Tōhoku-in Shokunin Uta-awase Emaki" (housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}, [[:en:Important Cultural Property of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]) depicts a [[:en:gambler|gambler]] who, despite being stri11ed of his possessions, still wears an eboshi.<ref>[http://webarchives.tnm.jp/imgsearch/show/C0004831 Image]</ref> This illustrates that the eboshi symbolized masculinity at the time, and removing or being forced to remove it was seen as a humiliating act, often leading to conflicts. Notable eccentricities such as {{ill|Hosokawa Masamoto|en|Hosokawa Masamoto|qid=Q5365082|lt=Hosokawa Masamoto|1=Hosokawa Masamoto}}'s refusal to wear eboshi were considered peculiar behavior. However, starting in the [[Sengoku period (Japan)|Sengoku period]], it became commonplace to expose the {{ill|mage|en|mage|qid=Q7876248|lt=mage|1=Mage}} (topknot) without headgear in daily life. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, as the custom of tying topknots faded, eboshi with a chin strap for securing it to the head became popular. Contrarily, early Meiji [[court nobles]] favored large eboshi. Eboshi-shaped helmets, or "eboshi-nari" kabuto, were so named due to their resemblance to eboshi. The design of the {{ill|Kokumin-fuku|en|Kokumin-fuku|qid=Q4118791|lt=Kokumin-fuku|1=Kokumin-fuku}} hat, established in {{ill|Shōwa|en|Shōwa|qid=Q398996|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa}} 15 (1940), is said to have been inspired by the eboshi. In modern Japan, eboshi are worn in contexts such as [[Shinto]] rituals and [[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] by [[Shinto priests]], [[:en:Sumo wrestling|Sumo wrestling]] {{ill|Gyōji|en|Gyōji|qid=Q907647|lt=Gyōji|1=Gyōji}}, [[gagaku]] costumes, period dramas, traditional events, and history-themed performances. == Crafting Process == Materials include {{ill|Washi|en|Washi|qid=Q846299|lt=Washi|1=Washi}} (Japanese paper), with pre-Meiji varieties being of superior quality. While Western paper may be used, washi is lighter and more durable due to its long fibers. Washi is layered with glue, crumpled to integrate the adhesive, and placed on a co11er mold embossed with "shibo" (eboshi folds). The mold is brushed with a tool called a "sasara" to enhance the folds. After drying, the material is treated with [[persimmon tannin]] and shaped into three-dimensional form using a metallic mold. Details such as the "face" of the eboshi and the edging are completed, and the surface is lacquered for the final touch.<ref>[http://www.athome.co.jp/tobira/41/ #041 Eboshi Artisan Yokkaichi Ken]</ref> The number of eboshi artisans is declining. == Eboshi Parent and Eboshi Child == In the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} {{ill|Coming of Age Day|en|Coming of Age Day|qid=Q483226|lt=Coming of Age Day|1=Coming of Age Day}} (coming-of-age ceremony), the adult guardian who oversaw the ritual for the new adult (eboshi child) was referred to as the "eboshi parent." This custom traces its roots to the Heian period's "crowning ceremony" (初冠, hatsu-kagami). While court nobles wore kanmuri (冠), the use of eboshi was introduced for this ceremony. After the ceremony, men would customarily wear a crown or eboshi to signify their induction into adult society. Notably, children of the Heian period would mimic adults by tying triangular cloths to their foreheads in place of eboshi during play. Over time, these triangular cloths evolved into part of the [[shinishōzoku]] (death attire), with their color changed to white, the traditional color of death attire. == Types of Eboshi == [[File:JidaiMatsuri_Shizukagozen.jpg|thumb|240px|'''Tate-eboshi''' (center, worn by a female) and '''Hari-eboshi''' (worn by male attendants)]] ; Tate-eboshi : The most formal type of eboshi. Paired with [[:en:Kariginu|Kariginu]] (or rarely [[nōshi]]), it has a cylindrical shape flattened from both sides. After the Muromachi period, box-shaped eboshi became more common. Today, [[Shinto priests]] often wear this style. Gold versions are worn by [[chigo]] (children participating in festivals), while matte versions are used for funerals. ; Ori-eboshi : Broadly refers to folded eboshi. Narrowly, it refers to eboshi folded for convenience, used by {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} and commoners, also called "samurai eboshi." Modern-day {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|qid=Q40561|lt=Sumo|1=Sumo}} {{ill|Gyōji|en|Gyōji|qid=Q907647|lt=Gyōji|1=Gyōji}} wear this type. ; Momi-eboshi : Made of thin cloth, dyed with [[:en:gallnut|gallnut]]s or lightly lacquered for softness. ; Naye-eboshi : Flexible and unvarnished, differing from momi-eboshi. Commonly worn under {{ill|Kabuto|en|Kabuto|qid=Q1720878|lt=Kabuto|1=Kabuto}} (helmets) by samurai or as daily headwear for men. ; Tetsu-eboshi : Iron eboshi. A surviving example, used by [[Tanzō]], is preserved at [[Tōkei Shrine]]. ; Kazuori-eboshi : Worn by {{ill|Cormorant_fishing|en|Cormorant_fishing|qid=Q1784476|lt=ukai|1=Cormorant fishing}} fishermen, it is made of a single piece of hemp to protect their hair from sparks. == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == External Links == {{commonscat|Eboshi|qq=}} * [http://www.kariginu.jp/kikata/2-2.htm Crowns and Eboshi - Kyōshō Shōzoku Kenkyūjo (Institute for Research in Traditional Court Dress)] {{Japanese-history-stub|qq=}} {{Fashion-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|烏帽子|version=99592805|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:烏帽子]]
